topic.name: Australian Cricket History
topic.description: The documents discuss significant events and figures in the history of Australian cricket, focusing on notable teams, players, and matches.

1: The Australian cricket team in England in 1948 was captained by Don Bradman.

Australia won the match by 125 runs to claim the title for the third time.

Document 7: ECW One Night Stand (2005) was a professional wrestling pay-per-view event produced by World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE).

topic.name: The Boat Race History
topic.description: An overview of the annual rowing competition between the Universities of Oxford and Cambridge, detailing its historical significance, results, and rivalry.

Document 1: The Boat Race is an annual side-by-side rowing race between crews from the Universities of Oxford and Cambridge along the River Thames.
The race was first held in 1829, and since 1845 has taken place on the 4.2-mile (6.8 km) Championship Course on the River Thames in southwest London.

topic.name: State Highways in the US
topic.description: The documents discuss various state highways across the United States, detailing their routes, history, and characteristics.

Document 1: M-58 is a 5.108-mile-long east – west state trunkline highway in the US state of Michigan
The Saginaw version was designated in 1971, and it is listed on the National Highway System.
Document 2: Park Road 3 is a 1.2-mile Park Road located near Fort Davis, in the U.S. state of Texas
The highway connects the Indian Lodge of Davis Mountains State Park to State Highway 118.
Document 3: Delaware Route 82 is a state highway in the northwest suburbs of Wilmington in New Castle County, Delaware
The route runs 5.51 miles from DE 52 near Greenville to the Pennsylvania border.
Document 4: K-1 is a 13.363-mile state highway in Comanche County in the U.S. state of Kansas
Its southern terminus is at the Oklahoma border south of Buttermilk.
Document 5: State Route 103 is a 0.225-mile-long urban minor arterial state highway in the U.S. state of Utah
It branches off from SR-126 in downtown Clearfield and extends east to Interstate 15.
Document 6: State Route 228 is a short north – south state highway in the U.S. state of Ohio
The southern terminus of the state route is at State Route 19.
Document 7: Delaware Route 5 is a 19.48-mile-long state highway in Sussex County, Delaware
The route runs north from River Road on the Indian River Bay in Oak Orchard.

topic.name: Naval Warfare History
topic.description: The documents discuss various naval vessels and their roles in historical military conflicts, primarily during World War I and World War II.

Document 1: HMS Ark Royal was the first ship in history designed and built as a seaplane carrier.
Ark Royal participated in the Gallipoli Campaign in early 1915 with her aircraft conducting aerial reconnaissance.
Document 2: The SS Gothenburg was a steamship that operated along the British and then later the Australian and New Zealand coastlines.
The ship was wrecked on the Great Barrier Reef north-west of Holbourne Island on 24 February 1875.
Document 3: Z10 Hans Lody was a Type 1934A-class destroyer built for Nazi Germany's Kriegsmarine in the mid-1930s.
The ship laid multiple offensive minefields off the English coast that claimed nine merchant ships.
Document 4: The Admiral-class battlecruisers were to have been a class of four British Royal Navy battlecruisers designed near the end of World War I.
Their designs were updated to incorporate the lessons from the Battle of Jutland.
Document 5: Gneisenau was a German capital ship, alternatively described as a battleship and battlecruiser, of Nazi Germany's Kriegsmarine.
The ship was armed with a main battery of nine 28 cm (11 in) C/34 guns in three triple turrets.
Document 6: The Borodino-class battlecruisers were a group of four battlecruisers ordered by the Imperial Russian Navy before World War I.
Construction of the ships was delayed as many domestic factories were overloaded with orders.
Document 7: Kongō was a warship of the Imperial Japanese Navy during World War I and World War II.
Kongō fought in a large number of major naval actions of the Pacific War during World War II.

topic.name: Historical Figures in Education and Politics
topic.description: This topic encompasses notable individuals who played significant roles in education and political governance in North America during the 18th and 19th centuries.

1: John J. Tigert was an American university president and U.S. Commissioner of Education.
Tigert is remembered as a forceful advocate for American public education.
2: Sir Francis Bernard was a British colonial administrator whose policies influenced opposition to Parliament leading to the American Revolution.
Bernard's actions as governor deepened divisions between the colonies and British Parliament.
3: Charles Wilson Cross was the first Attorney-General of Alberta and served in Canadian politics.
Cross was implicated in a scandal that affected his political career.
5: Philip Primrose was the fifth Lieutenant Governor of Alberta and served in the North-West Mounted Police.
Primrose held various positions in law enforcement and politics before his brief tenure as Lieutenant Governor.
6: Edward Pulsford was an Australian politician known for his free-trade advocacy and opposition to racial discrimination.
Pulsford's political career included significant opposition to the White Australia policy.

topic.name: Marine Animals and Their Habitats
topic.description: The documents discuss various species of marine animals, their characteristics, behaviors, and habitats.

Document 1: The spinner shark is a species of requiem shark, in the family Carcharhinidae, named for the spinning leaps it makes as a part of its feeding strategy.
This species occurs in tropical and warm temperate waters worldwide.
Document 2: The black drongo is a small Asian passerine bird of the drongo family Dicruridae.
It is common in open agricultural areas and light forest throughout its range.
Document 3: The pelagic stingray is characterized by the wedge-like shape of its pectoral fin disc, which is much wider than long.
This species is typically found in surface waters down to a depth of 100 m.
Document 4: The milk shark is a species of requiem shark, and part of the family Carcharhinidae.
This species is common near beaches and in estuaries.
Document 5: Portia labiata is a jumping spider found in Sri Lanka, India, Burma, Malaysia, Singapore.
All members of Portia have instinctive hunting tactics for their most common prey.
Document 6: Coffin birth is the expulsion of a nonviable fetus through the vaginal opening of the decomposing body of a deceased pregnant woman.
This kind of postmortem delivery occurs very rarely during the decomposition of a body.
Document 7: The coffin ray is a species of electric ray endemic to Australia.
It is a voracious predator that feeds mainly on benthic bony fishes.

topic.name: Historical Development of American Institutions
topic.description: The documents collectively explore the historical development and significance of various American institutions, including educational, sports, political, and economic entities.

Document 1: The history of Georgetown University spans nearly four hundred years
Georgetown's origins are in the establishment of the Maryland colony in the seventeenth-century.
Document 2: Three Rivers Stadium was a multi-purpose stadium located in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania from 1970 to 2000
It was home to the Pittsburgh Pirates of Major League Baseball (MLB) and the Pittsburgh Steelers of the National Football League (NFL).
Document 3: Robert Deniston Hume was a cannery owner, pioneer hatchery operator, politician, author

Document 4: Steve Beshear was the 61st Governor of Kentucky from 2007 to 2015
He served in the Kentucky House of Representatives from 1974 to 1979.
Document 5: Public Storage is an American international self storage company headquartered in Glendale, California
It is the largest brand of self-storage services in the US.
Document 6: Minneapolis is the largest city by population in the U.S. state of Minnesota
The origin and growth of the city was spurred by the proximity of Fort Snelling.
Document 7: Gavin Newsom is an American businessman and television personality who currently serves as the 49th Lieutenant Governor of California
Newsom began his political career in 1996 when San Francisco Mayor Willie Brown appointed him to serve on the city's Parking and Traffic Commission.

Document 2: The 2016 Winter Youth Olympics are scheduled in and around Lillehammer, Norway.
Nine competition and twelve non-competition venues are to be used.

topic.name: Tropical Cyclone Seasons
topic.description: The documents discuss various tropical cyclone seasons across different ocean basins, detailing the storms, their impacts, and seasonal characteristics.

1: The 2001 Atlantic hurricane season was a fairly active Atlantic hurricane season that produced 17 tropical cyclones
The season caused 105 fatalities, and $ 7.1 billion in damage.
2: The 1999 – 2000 South-West Indian Ocean tropical cyclone season was the first on record in which two storms struck Mozambique at tropical cyclone intensity
Collectively, the three storms killed at least 316 people.
3: The 2002 – 03 South Pacific cyclone season was the most active and longest tropical cyclone season since 1997 – 98
The season started earlier than normal, with two systems developing before the official start of the season.
4: Hurricane Bob was one of the costliest hurricanes in New England history
Bob made landfall twice in Rhode Island as a Category 2 hurricane.
5: The 1951 Atlantic hurricane season was the first hurricane season in which tropical cyclones were officially named
The season had the least tropical cyclone damage in the United States since the 1939 season.
6: The 1961 Atlantic hurricane season featured the highest number of major hurricanes until being tied by 2005
It was an above average season in terms of tropical storms.
7: The 1982 Atlantic hurricane season officially began on June 1, 1982 and lasted until November 30, 1982
Only six storms formed during this hurricane season.

topic.name: Natural Environments and Weather Phenomena
topic.description: This cluster discusses various natural environments, including parks, water bodies, and weather systems, highlighting their characteristics and interactions with human activities.

Document 1: Nairobi National Park is a national park in Kenya.
Despite its proximity to civilisation and relative small size for an African national park, Nairobi National Park boasts a large and varied wildlife population.
Document 2: Hurricane Patricia was the second-most intense tropical cyclone on record worldwide.
Patricia grew from a tropical storm to a Category 5 hurricane in just 24 hours.
Document 3: West Branch Fishing Creek is one of the northernmost major tributaries of Fishing Creek.
The creek's watershed has an area of 33.5 square miles, nearly all of which is forested land.
Document 4: A cold-core low, also known as an upper level low or cold-core cyclone, is a cyclone aloft which has an associated cold pool of air.
Cold lows can help spawn cyclones with significant weather impacts.
Document 5: In meteorology, a cyclone is a large scale air mass that rotates around a strong center of low atmospheric pressure.
Cyclogenesis describes the process of cyclone formation and intensification.
Document 6: Little Wapwallopen Creek is a tributary of the Susquehanna River in Luzerne County, Pennsylvania.
The watershed of the creek has an area of 39.5 square miles.
Document 7: A high-pressure area, high or anticyclone is a region where the atmospheric pressure at the surface of the planet is greater than its surrounding environment.
The strongest high-pressure areas are associated with cold air masses which push away out of polar regions during the winter.

topic.name: Science Fiction Television Series

topic.name: Diverse Flora and Fauna
topic.description: The documents explore various aspects of plant and animal life, highlighting their characteristics, habitats, and ecological significance.

1: Liliaceae consists of fifteen genera and about 705 known species of flowering plants.
Plants in this family have evolved with a fair amount of morphological diversity despite genetic similarity.
2: A Brumby is a free-roaming feral horse in Australia.
Brumbies are the descendants of escaped or lost horses, dating back in some cases to those belonging to the early European settlers.
3: In everyday usage, a vegetable is any part of a plant that is consumed by humans as food.
The original meaning of the word vegetable was to describe all types of plant.
4: Mount Kenya is the highest mountain in Kenya and the second-highest in Africa.
The forested slopes are an important source of water for much of Kenya.
5: Riesling is a white grape variety which originated in the Rhine region of Germany.
Riesling wines are usually varietally pure and are seldom oaked.
6: The flora of Scotland is an assemblage of native plant species including over 1,600 vascular plants.
Conservation of the natural environment is well developed and various organisations play an important role in the stewardship of the country’s flora.
7: The annelids are a large phylum, with over 17,000 extant species including ragworms, earthworms, and leeches.
Annelids are considered members of the Lophotrochozoa, a 'super-phylum' of protostomes that also includes molluscs, brachiopods, flatworms and nemerteans.

topic.name: Political Movements and Assassinations
topic.description: This topic encompasses various political movements, ideologies, and notable acts of political violence throughout history.

a Confederate sympathizer, vehement in his denunciation of Lincoln.

a militant opponent of Zionism in the 1920s and 1930s.

mobilized social networking websites and gained support from opposition societies.

advocates for the recognition of the Armenian Genocide and the right to reparations.
Document 7: Hezbollah is a Shi'a Islamist militant group and political party based in Lebanon
conceived by Muslim clerics and funded by Iran primarily to harass the Israeli occupation.

Document 7: The United States Special Operations Command (USSOCOM) is the Unified Combatant Command charged with overseeing the various Special Operations Component Commands
USSOCOM conducts several covert and clandestine missions.

topic.name: Major League Baseball Players
topic.description: The documents discuss the careers and achievements of various Major League Baseball players throughout history.

1: Jim Umbricht was an American professional baseball player.
He played Major League Baseball (MLB) between 1959 and 1963.
2: Hugh Daily was an Irish American professional right-handed pitcher who played six seasons.
He was successful as a starting pitcher early in his major league career.
3: Hack Wilson was an American Major League Baseball player who played 12 seasons.
His 1930 season with the Cubs is widely considered one of the most memorable individual single-season hitting performances.
4: Billy Pierce was an American starting pitcher in Major League Baseball between 1945 and 1964.
He was the team's star pitcher in the decade from 1952 to 1961.
5: Trevor Hoffman is an American former baseball relief pitcher who played 18 years in Major League Baseball.
He was the major leagues' first player to reach the 500- and 600-save milestones.
6: Dan Brouthers was an American first baseman in Major League Baseball whose career spanned the period from 1879 to 1896.
Recognized as the first great slugger in baseball history.
7: Will Middlebrooks is an American professional baseball third baseman for the Milwaukee Brewers.
He made his MLB debut with the Boston Red Sox on May 2, 2012.

topic.name: Railway Stations and Infrastructure
topic.description: The documents discuss various railway stations and their historical significance, operations, and developments in the UK and Norway.

Document 1: Clifton Down railway station is on the Severn Beach Line and serves the district of Clifton in Bristol, England.
The station was opened in 1874 by the Great Western and Midland Railways as part of the Clifton Extension Railway.
Document 2: The Oslo Tunnel is a 3,632-meter double-track railway tunnel which runs between Olav Kyrres plass and Oslo Central Station.
The tunnel opened on 1 June 1980, and made it possible to close Oslo V in 1989.
Document 3: The Manchester and Bolton Railway was a railway in the historic county of Lancashire, England, connecting Salford to Bolton.
The railway was opened in 1838 to passenger and freight services.
Document 4: Patchway railway station is on the South Wales Main Line, serving the Bristol suburbs of Patchway and Stoke Gifford in South Gloucestershire, England.
The station was opened by the Bristol and South Wales Union Railway in 1863.
Document 5: Embankment is a London Underground station in the City of Westminster, known by various names during its history.
The station has two entrances, one on Victoria Embankment and the other on Villiers Street.
Document 6: Lysaker Station is a railway station on the Drammen Line and Asker Line situated at Lysaker in Bærum, Norway.
The station was rebuilt again from 2006 to 2009 in which it was expanded from two to four tracks.

topic.name: Medieval European Conflicts
topic.description: The documents discuss various military conflicts and political dynamics in medieval Europe, focusing on the Byzantine Empire, the Bulgarian Empire, and the rise of regional powers.

1: The Battle of Kleidion took place on July 29, 1014 between the Byzantine Empire and the Bulgarian Empire.
The battle was a major defeat for the Bulgarians.
2: The Livonian War was fought for control of Old Livonia when the Tsardom of Russia faced off against a varying coalition.
The war involved multiple powers including Denmark, Sweden, and the Grand Duchy of Lithuania.
3: Æthelwig was an Abbot of Evesham before and during the Norman Conquest of England.
He was one of the few Englishmen trusted by the new King William the Conqueror.
4: Stephen Lekapenos was the second son of the Byzantine emperor Romanos I Lekapenos and co-emperor from 924 to 945.
He was overthrown and exiled by the legitimate emperor Constantine VII.
5: Basarab I was the first independent ruler of Wallachia and came into power before 1324.
He seized the Banate of Severin and raided the southern regions of the Kingdom of Hungary.
6: John Doukas was a senior military figure during the reign of Byzantine emperor Alexios I Komnenos.
He secured imperial possessions in the western Balkans against the Serbs.
7: Mary, Queen of Hungary, was the daughter of Louis the Great and faced challenges from Hungarian noblemen regarding her rule.
Her marriage was politically motivated to strengthen her position as queen.

topic.name: Australian Military History
topic.description: The documents discuss various aspects of the Australian military forces and their involvement in significant historical conflicts, particularly during World War I and World War II.

Document 1: Western Area Command was one of several geographically based commands raised by the Royal Australian Air Force (RAAF) during World War II.
Its aircraft conducted anti-submarine operations throughout the war.
Document 2: The 2nd Commando Regiment is an elite special forces unit of the Australian Army.
It has been involved in operations in East Timor, Iraq and Afghanistan.
Document 3: Lewis McGee was an Australian recipient of the Victoria Cross, the highest decoration for gallantry.
He was awarded the Victoria Cross for his actions in the Battle of Broodseinde.
Document 4: Brigadier Reginald Miles served in the New Zealand Military Forces during the First and Second World Wars.
He was awarded the Distinguished Service Order for his actions during the Spring Offensive.
Document 5: The 18th Battalion was a formation of the New Zealand Military Forces which served during the Second World War.
It participated in the Battle of Greece and later in the fighting on Crete.
Document 6: The 2nd Army Group was a Royal Yugoslav Army formation during the German-led Axis invasion of the Kingdom of Yugoslavia in April 1941.
It was responsible for the defence of the border with Hungary.
Document 7: Eric Lock was a British Royal Air Force fighter pilot and flying ace of the Second World War.
Lock became the RAF's most successful Allied pilot during the Battle of Britain.

topic.name: Music Albums and Artists

Document 7: The Slip is the seventh studio album by American industrial rock band Nine Inch Nails

The song received mixed reviews from critics, who were ambivalent towards its composition.

The song garnered acclaim from music critics, who called it an improvement over the Pretenders' original version.

A massive candlelight vigil took place in the vicinity of it.

topic.name: American Revolutionary War Events
topic.description: Key events and battles during the American Revolutionary War that shaped the conflict between American colonists and British forces.

Document 1: The Boston Massacre, known as the Incident on King Street by the British, was an incident on March 5, 1770, in which British Army soldiers killed five male civilians and injured six others.
The incident was heavily propagandized by leading Patriots, such as Paul Revere and Samuel Adams, to fuel animosity toward the British authorities.
Document 2: The Siege of Fort St. Jean was conducted by American Brigadier General Richard Montgomery on the town and fort of Saint-Jean in the British province of Quebec during the American Revolutionary War.
The fall of Fort St. Jean opened the way for the American army to march on Montreal.
Document 3: The Battle of Long Island, also known as the Battle of Brooklyn, was the first major battle of the American Revolutionary War to take place after the United States declared its independence.
It was a victory for the British Army and the beginning of a successful campaign that gave them control of the strategically important city of New York.

topic.description: The documents discuss various NASCAR and Formula One races, detailing race results, drivers, and significant events during the races.

topic.name: Historic Landmarks and Architecture
topic.description: The documents discuss various historic landmarks and architectural structures across different locations in the United States and beyond.

Document 1: The Burnside Fountain is a non-functioning drinking fountain at the southeast corner of Worcester Common in Worcester, Massachusetts.
The fountain was commissioned in 1905 by the city of Worcester after Harriet F. Burnside bequeathed US $ 5,000 to create a fountain.
Document 2: Falkner Island Light, also known as the Faulkner Island Lighthouse, is a lighthouse in Connecticut, United States.
The lighthouse was constructed in 1802 and commissioned by President Thomas Jefferson.
Document 3: Central Park is a 51-storey office tower in Perth, Western Australia.
Upon its completion in 1992, the tower became the tallest building in Perth.
Document 4: Barrington Civic Center Historic District is a historic district in Barrington, Rhode Island on County Road.
The Barrington Civic Center Historic District was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1976.
Document 5: The Gurdon Bill Store is located in Ledyard, Connecticut.
The Gurdon Bill Store was added to the National Register of Historic Places on April 12, 1982.
Document 6: The architecture of Leeds encompasses a wide range of architectural styles and notable buildings.
Most of the current buildings in Leeds are the product of the Industrial Revolution and post war regeneration.
Document 7: The Indiana Governor's Residence is the official home of the family of the Governor of Indiana.
Designed by Scott Wadley, the English Tudor home was built in 1928.

topic.name: State Highways and Routes
topic.description: The documents discuss various state highways and routes in North America, detailing their connections, historical development, and geographical significance.

Document 1: Highway 16 is a provincial paved highway in the Canadian province of Saskatchewan.
The highway runs from the Alberta boundary in Lloydminster to the Manitoba boundary near Marchwell.
Document 2: There have been nine business routes for Interstate 75 in the US state of Michigan.
These highways are former routings of I-75 's predecessor highways in the state.
Document 3: Maryland Route 213 is a 68.25-mile state highway located on the Eastern Shore of Maryland.
The route runs from MD 662 in Wye Mills north to the Pennsylvania border.
Document 4: There have been seven business routes of US Highway 31 in the state of Michigan.
They connect the freeway mainline of US 31 to the various downtowns of cities bypassed by US 31.
Document 5: Maryland Route 313 is a state highway located on the Eastern Shore of Maryland.
The 75.72-mile route runs from U.S. Route 50 in Mardela Springs north to MD 213.
Document 6: M-52 is a north – south state trunkline highway in the US state of Michigan.
The southern terminus is on the Ohio border, where it continues as State Route 109.
Document 7: Maryland Route 16 is a state highway in the U.S. state of Maryland.
MD 16 connects Cambridge with several communities in northern Dorchester County.

topic.name: Scientific Concepts and Theories
topic.description: The documents explore various scientific concepts and theories across different fields such as physics, ethics, medicine, and communication.

1: Liquid crystals ( LCs ) are matter in a state which has properties between those of conventional liquids and those of solid crystals.
There are many different types of liquid-crystal phases, which can be distinguished by their different optical properties.
2: Kantian ethics refers to a deontological ethical theory ascribed to the German philosopher Immanuel Kant.
Central to Kant's construction of the moral law is the categorical imperative, which acts on all people, regardless of their interests or desires.
3: Tracheal intubation, usually simply referred to as intubation, is the placement of a flexible plastic tube into the trachea (windpipe) to maintain an open airway.
It is frequently performed in critically injured, ill, or anesthetized patients to facilitate ventilation of the lungs.
4: The kilogram or kilogramme (SI unit symbol: kg) is the base unit of mass in the International System of Units (SI).
The gram, 1/1000 of a kilogram, was provisionally defined in 1795 as the mass of one cubic centimeter of water at the melting point of ice.
5: Nonviolent communication (abbreviated NVC, also called compassionate communication) is a communication process developed by Marshall Rosenberg beginning in the 1960s.
NVC proposes that if people can identify their needs, the needs of others, and the feelings that surround these needs, harmony can be achieved.
6: In particle physics, quantum electrodynamics (QED) is the relativistic quantum field theory of electrodynamics.
QED mathematically describes all phenomena involving electrically charged particles interacting by means of exchange of photons.

topic.name: Cultural and Historical Artifacts
topic.description: The documents discuss various cultural and historical artifacts and sites, highlighting their significance and impact on society.

Document 7: Okay Hot-Shot, Okay! is a 1963 pop art painting by Roy Lichtenstein that uses his Ben-Day dots style.
It was inspired by panels from four different comic books.

topic.name: Naval Warships History
topic.description: This topic encompasses the historical development, design, and service records of various naval warships from different countries during the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

Document 1: HMS Swiftsure was the lead ship of the Swiftsure-class pre-dreadnought battleships.
Swiftsure was assigned to convoy escort duties in the Atlantic from early 1916 until she was paid off in April 1917.
Document 2: The French destroyer Chacal was the name ship of her class of destroyers built for the French Navy during the 1920s.
Chacal was crippled by German bombers and artillery on 23 / 24 May.
Document 3: SMS Wittelsbach was the lead ship of the Wittelsbach class of pre-dreadnought battleships of the Kaiserliche Marine.
Wittelsbach served in the I Squadron of the German fleet for the majority of her career.
Document 4: Hiei was the second and last vessel of the Kongō-class ironclad corvettes built for the Imperial Japanese Navy.
Hiei participated in the Battle of Weihaiwei and the invasion of Formosa in 1895.
Document 5: HMS Iron Duke was the last of four Audacious-class central battery ironclads built for the Royal Navy.
Iron Duke was converted into a coal hulk a decade later and continued in that role until 1906.
Document 6: The Imperator Aleksandr II-class battleships were built for the Imperial Russian Navy in the 1880s.
They were optimized for ramming.
Document 7: Francesco Morosini was an ironclad battleship built in the 1880s and 1890s for the Italian Regia Marina.
She was quickly rendered obsolescent by the new pre-dreadnought battleships being laid down.

topic.name: Amusement Parks and Attractions
topic.description: This topic encompasses various amusement park rides, particularly roller coasters, and their historical significance and characteristics.

Document 2: The Giant Dipper is a historic wooden roller coaster located at the Santa Cruz Beach Boardwalk.
It has received several awards such as being named a National Historic Landmark.
Document 3: Kumba is a Bolliger & Mabillard sit down roller coaster located at Busch Gardens Tampa Bay.
It features a total of seven inversions across the 3-minute ride.
Document 4: Scream is a floorless roller coaster at Six Flags Magic Mountain.
The ride consists of a series of roller coaster elements including seven inversions.

Document 7: El Toro is a wooden roller coaster at Six Flags Great Adventure in Jackson, New Jersey.
It had the steepest drop of any wooden roller coaster in the world at 76 degrees.

topic.name: Video Game Music and Development

Document 4: The development of The Last of Us began after Uncharted 2 : Among Thieves ' release in October 2009.

topic.name: Geography and Waterways of the UK

topic.name: Atlantic Hurricane History
topic.description: This topic encompasses the historical accounts and impacts of tropical cyclones in the Atlantic region, particularly focusing on their formation, development, and effects on land.

Document 1: Hurricane Caroline was one of two tropical cyclones to affect northern Mexico during the 1975 Atlantic hurricane season.
There were only two deaths from Caroline, both indirect.
Document 2: Tropical Storm Fran was one of the deadliest tropical cyclones in the history of Cape Verde.
The storm caused no impact outside of Cape Verde.
Document 3: The effects of Hurricane Dean in Mexico were more severe than anywhere else in the storm's path.
Hurricane Dean, the most intense storm of the 2007 Atlantic hurricane season, formed in the Atlantic Ocean west of Cape Verde.
Document 4: The 1855 Atlantic hurricane season featured tropical cyclone landfalls in the Gulf Coast of the United States, the Greater Antilles, and Mexico.
Operationally, another tropical storm was believed to have existed offshore Atlantic Canada.
Document 5: Hurricane Cosme was a minimal hurricane that threatened Hawaii in mid-July 2007.
Because Cosme stayed far from land, effects were mostly minor.
Document 6: Hurricane Newton was one of the few tropical cyclones that were intercepted by Hurricane Hunter flights during the moderately active 1986 Pacific hurricane season.
Damage in Mexico was fairly minor, and no fatalities were reported in association with Newton.
Document 7: Hurricane Ella was the strongest hurricane on record in Canadian waters.
In North Carolina, the National Hurricane Center issued a hurricane watch due to the large influx of people expected during the holiday weekend.
